advaita.rendering: Or what further use to you, Arjuna, is this elaborate enumeration of partial knowing? I sustain this entire universe by pervading and firmly upholding it through a single fragment of myself — a single pada (quarter), as the śruti declares: 'pādo'sya viśvā bhūtāni' (Taittirīya Āraṇyaka 3.12). The remainderless knowing is this alone: Brahman, of which all beings are but one quarter, while three quarters abide as the immortal in the beyond.
advaita.key_divergence: Śaṅkara reads ekāṃśa strictly through the Puruṣasūkta's pāda-logic: creation exhausts only one fourth of Brahman; the infinite remainder is nirguṇa, untouched, transcendent. The 'support' (viṣṭabhya) is not relational governance but the universal substratum-presence of the ātman.
advaita.bhāṣya_anchor: ekāṃśena ekāvayavena ekāpādena sarvabhūtasvarūpeṇa — tathā ca mantravārṇaḥ pādo'sya viśvā bhūtāni

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: What need is there for all this extended knowing? I abide, having upheld this entire universe — gross and subtle, in causal and effected states, conscious and inert — in such a way that neither its existence nor its activities ever exceed my saṃkalpa (sovereign will). And this upholding is by an infinitesimal fraction of my infinite power, as Parāśara declared: 'of one in whom this universal power rests as but a fraction of a fraction' (Viṣṇu Purāṇa 1.9.53).
viśiṣṭādvaita.key_divergence: Rāmānuja emphasizes ayutāyutāṃśa — the cosmos is sustained by a fraction-of-a-fraction of Bhagavān's śakti. The universe is real, the jīvas are real, but both are the body (śarīra) of Īśvara whose sovereignty is absolute. Governance is active, personal, and relational — not mere substratum-presence.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: yathā matsaṃkalpaṃ na ativarteta tathā mama mahimnaḥ ayutāyutāṃśena viṣṭabhya aham avasthitaḥ

dvaita.rendering: This question 'what use is further knowing?' is posed not to dismiss prior knowledge as fruitless but to signal what is supremely essential: one who does not know this Lord in his distinct, qualified fullness (savīśeṣa-yukta) — how can such a one be liberated from bondage? The specification 'for you, Arjuna' signals that you are worthy of this supreme fruit. The word 'kim' here signals excellence, not negation — as in the Vedic usage 'if passions exist, what use is austerity; if they are absent, what use is austerity?' Bhagavān's sovereignty pervades all: 'yo māṃ paśyati sarvatra' (BG 6.30).
dvaita.key_divergence: Madhva refuses to read ekāṃśa as a Vedāntic sub-divisional metaphysic. His emphasis is on the Lord's savīśeṣatā — his fully qualified, personally distinct omnipresence. Liberation requires knowing Hari as wholly other than the jīva, not as the jīva's inner identity. The 'kim' is a rhetorical device signaling supreme value, not dismissal.
dvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: ajñātvainaṃ savīśeṣayuktaṃ devaṃ varaṃ ko vimucyeta bandhāt — prādhānyaṃ ca siddham ekatra darśanāt sarvatra bhagavad-darśanasya

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Thus, having enumerated the vibhūtis individually, the Lord now gathers the whole in a single conclusive statement. What use to you is all this detailed knowing? I, Puruṣottama, the supreme sovereign — by a single fragment of my imperishable (akṣara) majesty, in my adhyātma-form — uphold, pervade, govern, and sustain this entire universe of the conscious and the inert, in its subtle and manifest states. Know this as my yoga, my sovereign lordship. By saying 'a single fragment sustains all,' the Lord both removes the anxiety of infinite enumeration and reveals the true measure of his mahātmya.
śuddhādvaita.key_divergence: Vallabha foregrounds ekāṃśa as akṣara-mahimā — the imperishable, untranscended greatness of Kṛṣṇa acting in his adhyātma-rūpa. The entire cosmos is Kṛṣṇa's līlā-body, held by his own will-power, not by some impersonal substrate. The closing 'know this as my yoga' directs the devotee toward rasa-recognition, not philosophical analysis.
śuddhādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: ekāṃśenākṣaramahimnā adhyātmarūpeṇa kṛtsnam idaṃ cetanācetanātmakaṃ jagat viṣṭabhya vidhṛtya vyāpya niyamya ca sthito'smīti yogaṃ mamaiśvaryaṃ jānīhi

bhakti.rendering: Or rather — what use to you is so much fragmentary, piecemeal knowing? Let this single insight suffice: I alone stand here having upheld and pervaded this entire universe through just one fragment, one portion of myself. There is nothing whatsoever apart from me — as the śruti confirms: 'pādo'sya viśvā bhūtāni.' Therefore, cultivate equanimity of vision everywhere, seeing the one Self in all.
bhakti.key_divergence: Śrīdhara reads the verse as a practical instruction toward samadṛṣṭi (equal vision). The 'what use is more knowing?' is a gentle redirect: instead of cataloguing distinctions, see the undivided one. His reading bridges jñāna and bhakti by making the cosmological claim immediately actionable as a meditative discipline.
bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: yad idaṃ sarvaṃ jagad ekāṃśenaikadeśamātreṇa viṣṭabhya dhṛtvā vyāpyeti vā aham eva sthitaḥ na madvyatiriktaṃ kiñcid asti

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Having thus enumerated the vibhūtis individually, the Lord now states the whole in summary. Or — on the other view — what use to you, Arjuna, is this residual, partial knowing? I alone stand here, having upheld and pervaded this entire universe through a single fragment, a single portion; nothing exists apart from me — as the śruti proclaims: 'pādo'sya viśvā bhūtāni, tripād asyāmṛtaṃ divi.' Therefore, abandon this fractured vision and cultivate everywhere the single seeing of me. My heart, having once tasted even a drop of honey falling from the lotus of your feet, reels intoxicated again and again.
advaita-bhakti.key_divergence: Madhusūdana holds both poles simultaneously: he affirms Advaita's śruti-grounded non-duality (nothing exists apart from Brahman) and then pivots to the personal bhakti of rasa — the closing verse is his own composition expressing the devotee's maddened love. The intellectual closure of the chapter is made to serve a devotional opening, not a philosophical terminus.
advaita-bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: idaṃ kṛtsnaṃ sarvaṃ jagad ekāṃśena ekadeśamātreṇa viṣṭabhya vidhṛtya vyāpya vā aham eva sthito na madvyatiriktaṃ kiñcid asti — tatra tripādas amṛtam iti śruteḥ — tasmāt kim anena paricchinna-darśanena sarvatra mad-dṛṣṭim eva kurv ity abhiprāyaḥ

so_what_questions: If the entire cosmos is sustained by one fragment (ekāṃśa) of Bhagavān, what is the status of the cosmos itself — is it real, partial, or a projection of surplus infinity?

The verse closes Chapter 10 by withdrawing the very enumeration it just completed: what does it mean that a text names 60+ vibhūtis and then says 'but what use is all that knowing'? Is incompletability itself the teaching?

Each school locates the ekāṃśa differently: impersonal pāda for Śaṅkara, fraction-of-fraction for Rāmānuja, akṣara-mahimā for Vallabha. How does the choice of cosmological model determine the shape of the sādhaka's (practitioner's) path?
The verse is directed at Arjuna personally ('tava Arjuna'). Madhva reads this as a signal of adhikāra — Arjuna's fitness for this fruit. What does it mean to be worthy of a cosmological statement?
Śrīdhara and Madhusūdana both conclude with samadṛṣṭi as the practical upshot. How does 'seeing equally everywhere' function differently in a non-dual system versus a devotional one?

The verse implies that a partial inventory of the divine (vibhūti-enumeration) is less useful than a single structural insight (ekāṃśena sthitaḥ). What does this suggest about the relationship between religious data and religious understanding?
The Puruṣasūkta's pāda-schema is invoked by both Śaṅkara and Madhusūdana. How does a Vedic cosmogonic hymn function as an exegetical anchor — and what is at stake in that interpretive move?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself cataloguing grievances, achievements, or fears in obsessive detail, recognize this as avidyā-driven fragmentation. The śruti's pāda-insight applies: your entire life-narrative is at most one quarter of what you are. The untouched remainder neither grieves nor grasps. Practice withdrawing from the enumeration into that remainder — not as escapism but as the only stable ground from which action becomes clean.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Notice that you never truly exceed the field that has been given to you — your body, your relationships, your capacities are sustained by a will larger than your saṃkalpa. The Viśiṣṭādvaita reading invites you to treat every constraint (illness, limitation, loss) not as obstruction but as the precise shape of Bhagavān's governance for you. Kainkarya (service) within your actual conditions is the practice — not the conditions you wish you had.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's reading insists: do not collapse the distinction between the one who sustains and the one who is sustained. In practical terms, this means refusing the spiritual ego-move of identifying yourself with the sustaining power. Your sādhana is precisely in recognizing your dependence — 'I am held, not the holder' — and acting from that recognition. Hari's omnipresence is seen (BG 6.30) not felt as merger.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's puṣṭi-reading says the whole cosmos is Kṛṣṇa's play-body sustained by his own delight. In daily life, this translates to a radical reframing of burden: the weight you carry is not yours to carry alone — it is already held by the one whose fragment-power sustains galaxies. Receive each moment as prasāda (gift-of-grace), not as task-to-be-managed. The exhaustion of self-reliance is itself the invitation into this recognition.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's practical pivot — 'cultivate equal vision everywhere' — is the most directly applicable. When you are stuck in sectarian, competitive, or comparative seeing (this person is better, this situation is worse), the ekāṃśa-teaching offers a reset: the same one fragment is the substrate of the person you fear, the work you avoid, the pleasure you chase. Equal vision is not indifference — it is recognition of the common ground beneath all difference.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's synthesis closes the chapter not with a philosophical proposition but with a devotee's intoxication. The everyday application is this: intellectual closure of a question (Arjuna has now received the complete vibhūti-yoga) need not produce satisfaction — it can produce wonder. Let understanding ripen into longing. The honey-drop verse at the end is not decoration; it models the only response adequate to having understood that the whole cosmos is held by a fragment.

self_assessment: All six renderings anchored in supplied bhāṣya prose with real doctrinal divergence; no bhāṣya flagged absent; ekāṃśa polysemy (pāda-substratum vs fraction-of-fraction vs akṣara-mahimā vs samadṛṣṭi-pivot) preserved across all six schools without collapse.
